coredata.dc:title: A dual-functional fluorescence probe CDs@ZIF-90 for highly specific detection of Al3+ and Hg2+ in environmental water samples

coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the escalating water pollution has resulted in serious harm to human health and ecological environment due to the excessive discharge of toxic metal ions such as Al3+ and Hg2+. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a simple, efficient, and rapid detection method for monitoring the levels of the metal ions in water environment to ensure public health and ecological safety. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) containing heteroatom Si were successfully synthesized by the solvothermal method. Subsequently, a novel dual-functional fluorescent sensor (CDs@ZIF-90) was constructed by integrating CDs with zeolitic imidazolate framework-90 (ZIF-90). The fluorescent composite CDs@ZIF-90 showed outstanding optical properties and excellent structural and luminescence stability in aqueous medium. Particularly, its fluorescence at 453 nm can be remarkably enhanced by Al3+ and quenched upon exposure to Hg2+. As a result, the CDs@ZIF-90 was applied in sensitive and selective determination of Al3+ and Hg2+ ions with wide linear ranges (1–200 μM and 0.05–240 μM) and low detection limits (0.81 μM and 19.6 nM). Moreover, a convenient and rapid fluorescence test strip was also successfully prepared for visual detection of Al3+ and Hg2+ ions. This work is the first try to use the CDs@ZIF-90 fluorescence sensing material for highly sensitive and selective determination of Al3+ and Hg2+ based on “turn-on” and “turn-off” dual modes, respectively and it provides a new idea for monitoring quality of drinking water and environmental water. It is of great significance for human health and environmental protection.

head.ce:title: A dual-functional fluorescence probe CDs@ZIF-90 for highly specific detection of Al3+ and Hg2+ in environmental water samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, the escalating water pollution has resulted in serious harm to human health and ecological environment due to the excessive discharge of toxic metal ions such as Al3+ and Hg2+. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a simple, efficient, and rapid detection method for monitoring the levels of the metal ions in water environment to ensure public health and ecological safety. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) containing heteroatom Si were successfully synthesized by the solvothermal method. Subsequently, a novel dual-functional fluorescent sensor (CDs@ZIF-90) was constructed by integrating CDs with zeolitic imidazolate framework-90 (ZIF-90). The fluorescent composite CDs@ZIF-90 showed outstanding optical properties and excellent structural and luminescence stability in aqueous medium. Particularly, its fluorescence at 453 nm can be remarkably enhanced by Al3+ and quenched upon exposure to Hg2+. As a result, the CDs@ZIF-90 was applied in sensitive and selective determination of Al3+ and Hg2+ ions with wide linear ranges (1–200 μM and 0.05–240 μM) and low detection limits (0.81 μM and 19.6 nM). Moreover, a convenient and rapid fluorescence test strip was also successfully prepared for visual detection of Al3+ and Hg2+ ions. This work is the first try to use the CDs@ZIF-90 fluorescence sensing material for highly sensitive and selective determination of Al3+ and Hg2+ based on “turn-on” and “turn-off” dual modes, respectively and it provides a new idea for monitoring quality of drinking water and environmental water. It is of great significance for human health and environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the increasingly serious issue of water pollution, it is very urgent to construct the analysis and detection technologies toward the targeted pollutants with high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, good portability, short response time, and low cost. In particular, water pollution problems caused by highly toxic heavy metal ions are particularly prominent. Especially, mercury, as an extremely dangerous and ubiquitous pollutant in the global environment, is widespread in water, air, soil and food. When ingested in excess, they will be enriched in the food chain and cause a number of health problems to organisms [1,2]. More seriously, microorganisms in water can easily convert inorganic mercury further into organic mercury such as methyl mercury. Unfortunately, methyl mercury is more toxic than inorganic Hg2+ because it readily crosses biological membranes, including the blood-brain barrier, and accumulates in the brain system and nervous system [3]. Therefore, the excessive presence of Hg2+ can cause serious harm to the nervous system, kidneys, and lungs [4]. In addition, aluminum, the most abundant metal in the earth's crust, is used in a wide range and plays an important role in people's daily lives, such as cooking utensils, beverage cans and electrical wires. Due to acidic rain and human activities, it widely exists within environment. To the best of our knowledge, the excessive accumulation of Al3+ in human body may lead to many diseases, such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's syndrome and other neurodegenerative diseases [5,6]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop highly sensitive and selective analysis methods for monitoring the levels of these metal ions in environmental water samples in order to safeguard human health.

In recent years, a large amount of analytical methods have been developed to detect the concentration of metal ions in aqueous solutions, such as electrochemistry [7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [8], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) [10], and so on. Comparing the advantages and shortcomings of the above methods, it is found that although they are sensitive and accurate, these methods require complex sample pretreatment process, expensive instruments and time-consuming operations, which tremendously limits their practical applications [11,12]. Markedly, fluorescence sensors have attracted a great deal of attention due to their conspicuous advantages, such as simple operation, low cost, high reponse speed and high sensitivity [13,14]. So far a variety of materials have been developed as fluorescent sensors for detecting metal ions, including organic molecules [15], carbon dots [16] and luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) [17].

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs), a kind of multifunctional hybrid materials with a porous network structure, have provided a versatile platform for the development of emerging fluorescent sensors [18–20]. MOFs are formed by self-assembly of metal ions or metal clusters with organic ligands via coordination bond, so their luminescence properties may come from inorganic components (metal ions) and organic components (organic ligands) [21,22]. Prominently, zeolite imidazole frameworks (ZIFs), a subclass of MOFs, due to their attractive pore size and excellent thermal and chemical stability, are more often functionalized to build good sensing platforms [23]. And combination of MOFs with other luminescent materials can be also adopted to construct fluorescent sensors. For instance, by encapsulating carbon dots into MOFs, the tunable luminescence properties of the carbon dots with the advantages of the host MOFs in terms of accumulating the target analytes might be efficiently realized, resulting in sensing platforms with high sensitivity and selectivity [24,25].

Carbon dots (CDs), the protagonist of luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials, have gained tremendous attention for their excellent optical properties, high water solubility and biocompatibility [26]. These inherent advantages of CDs make them have more excellent optical performances compared with conventional fluorescent materials including organic dyes and rare earth metals, so that they have been widely used in many fields [27] such as chemical sensing [28], cell imaging [29,30], anti-counterfeiting [31], optoelectronic devices [32], and medical diagnosis [33,34]. However, in spite of above characteristics and wide applications, conventionally synthesized CDs still have some limitations. Easy agglomeration because of too small size will lead to a decrease of the fluorescence intensity to some extent [35,36]. Encapsulating CDs within MOFs are able to mitigate effectively the agglomeration effect of CDs owing to the porous nature of MOFs, while chemical bonding can improve the structural stability of CDs composites. So far, composition of CDs with MOFs has become one of the most efficient strategies to construct CDs-based fluorescence sensors. For example, Li et al. [37] and Hu et al. [38] respectively incorporated CDs into ZIF-90 to construct fluorescence-secondary scattering ratiometric sensors, realizing the detection of phosphate and sulfite/sulfate. It has been reported that many methods can be used to prepare CDs@MOFs composites, such as one-step method [39], physical mixing [40] and chemical bonding [41]. Relatively speaking, chemical bonding method is more popular by which more stable CDs@MOFs composites with superior optical perporties are able to be acquired [42]. Currently, the commonly used chemical synthesis methods include hydrothermal and solvothermal methods. Usually, the preparation process is always accompanied by a number of chemical reactions such as dehydration and condensation. And the functional groups of CDs and MOFs undergo chemical reactions to form firm covalent bonds, leading to structurally stable composite materials CDs@MOFs, which efficiently improves the application performances of CDs-polymer composites. For example, Fu et al. prepared FA-CDs@EuMOFs composites by a solvothermal procedure through incorporating pre-synthesized amide-based nitrogen-doped CDs (FA-CDs) into the precursors for preparing Eu-MOFs. The dehydration condensation of –NH2 of FA-CDs with –COOH of Eu-MOFs leads to strong chemical bond C–N and resulting firm encapsulation of the CDs in the cavity of Eu-MOFs [41]. Ren et al. [43] respectively used two methods to prepare two different structures of CDs@MOFs composites. They first prepared CMOFs by directly adding CDs into the reaction mixture during MOF synthesis by a one-pot method, then synthesized CDs@MOFs by mixing CDs and as-prepared MOFs via a two-step method. In these two composites, –COOH in MOFs dehydrates with –NH2 of CDs surface to form amide bond and the surface state change of CDs significantly improve the optical properties of products. In addition, in a N-CDs@MIPs composite synthesized by a reverse microemulsion method, N-containing CDs combined with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) prepared with (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane, tetraethoxysilane, and poly dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride through strong Si–O–Si, Si–O–C covalent bonds [44], which significantly promotes the sensing of the composite toward genistein.

Based on the above discussion, this paper developed a dual-functional fluorescent probe based on MOFs through an in-situ growth strategy. This approach offered several advantages, including simple probe preparation, rapid fluorescence response, high sensitivity and selectivity and wide linear range toward detection of the targeted analytes. In this protocol, CDs containing Si atoms were synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method, which were then incorporated into the framework of ZIF-90 to successfully obtain a novel CDs-functionalized ZIF-90 material (CDs@ZIF-90). The selected CDs can form highly stable composite with ZIF-90 through multiple interactions including covalent bond and hydrogen bonds between -SiOH, –NH2 and –COOH on CDs and –CHO and –COOH in ZIF-90. Upon addition of Al3+ and Hg2+, respectively, the proposed sensor CDs@ZIF-90 at once exhibits opposite fluorescence responses signals (Scheme 1 ). Although Wang et al. [45] have reported a fluorescent sensor NCDs@ZIF-90 for Al3+, the sensitivity of this sensor is unsatisfactory. At the same time, it can also only be used for the detection of Al3+ in ethanol media. But as we know, a wide range of metal ions always coexist in aqueous environments. In comparison, the sensor we developed is superior to NCDs@ZIF-90 in terms of sensitivity, environmental sustainability, etc. This method has several advantages, including simple probe preparation, rapid fluorescence reaction, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and a wide linear range for detection of target analytes. Up to now, no MOFs-based dual-functional fluorescent sensors have been reported to for detection of Al3+ and Hg2+ respectively based “turn-on” and “turn-off” modes. So this work is interesting and promising. Furthermore, we have successfully developed fluorescent test strips for the visual identification of Al3+ and Hg2+ in water, which lays a solid foundation for on-site detection of both elements. What's more, the sensor has successfully been applied to detect Al3+ and Hg2+ in samples of Yellow River water (Lanzhou reach, Gansu Province) and Tap water with exceptional selectivity, low detection limit and remarkable accuracy, demonstrating its high practicality for environmental water sample analysis.
The CDs were synthesized according to the procedure described in previously published literature [46] and the detailed synthesis procedure can be found in the Supplementary material.

ZIF-90 was synthesized using a modified literature method [47] and the detailed process is provided in the Supplementary material. The synthesis process of CDs@ZIF-90 is similar to that of ZIF-90 except for addition of CDs in the reaction mixture. Specifically, a DMF solution of Zn(CH3COOH)2·2H2O (2 mL, 0.1 M) was added to a DMF solution of imidazole-2-carboxyaldehyde (ICA) (2 mL, 0.2 M). Then, 0.0128 g of the synthesized CDs was added to the mixed solution. After stirring for 5 min, 10 mL of DMF was added and the reaction was continued for 1 h. After the reaction finished, the product was centrifugated and washed several times with methanol and deionized water. The obtained product was dried in vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and Al3+ in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the selectivity of the probe, CDs@ZIF-90 (0.5 mg) was dispersed 5 mL of 1 × 10−2 M cationic solution (Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Al3+ and Ba2+) prepared by corresponding nitrates. The mixture was sonicated for 5 min at room temperature and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. Subsequently, different concentrations of Hg2+ and Al3+ were added to the CDs@ZIF-90 suspension, respectively. After sonication reaction for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. Finally, the anti-interference ability of the probe was further investigated by mixing Hg2+ or Al3+ with other coexisting metal interfering ions in CDs@ZIF-90 suspension, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent strips for detection of Hg2+ and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to facilitate on-site detection of Al3+ and Hg2+, we prepared a 1 cm wide test strip using CDs/ZIF-90 as the sensing material. The preparation involved dispersing 10 mg of CDs/ZIF-90 in 20 mL of deionized water followed by a sonication treatment for uniform dispersion. Subsequently, the blank test strip was immersed in the suspension of CDs/ZIF-90 for approximately 24 h. After taken out, the test strip was dried at 60 °C under vacuum condition for 12 h to obtain blue fluorescent test strip. The color changes of the test strips in solutions with varying concentrations of Al3+ and Hg2+ ions were ultimately observed under UV-lamp.

To further investigate the practical application value of the probe, the Al3+ and Hg2+ in the water samples were determined by the standard addition method by employing laboratory tap water and Yellow River water as actual samples. First, the water samples were filtered to remove any insoluble impurities. Then different concentrations of Al3+ and Hg2+ standard solutions were added, and their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded.

CDs@ZIF-90 was synthesized from ZIF-90 precursor and fluorescent guest CDs. The morphologies and structures of the synthesized CDs, ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). TEM images of CDs indicated that most of them were quasi-spherical and uniformly dispersive in Fig. 1 a–c. From SEM images of ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 shown in Fig. 1d and e, it can be observed that the surface of polyhedral ZIF-90 is uniform and smooth. Fig. 1f and g manifested that the polyhedral morphology of ZIF-90 was well maintained after introduction of CDs, but its surface has become rougher. The elemental mapping of CDs@ZIF-90 convincingly demonstrated the existence of C, N, O, Zn and Si elements (Fig. 1h). Evidently, these elements in CDs@ZIF-90 were uniformly distributed. The contents of C, N, O, Si and Zn elements in the whole matrix were 45.66 %, 12.96 %, 14.50 %, 4.54 % and 22.34 %, respectively (Fig. S1). These results effectively proved that the successful preparation of CDs@ZIF- 90.

The crystalline properties of the materials were evaluated by Powder X-ray Diffraction (PXRD). As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the CDs@ZIF-90 (blue) showed similar peaks to those of pure ZIF-90 (red), which were also consistent with the simulated results (black). These results indicated that the introduction of CDs didn't cause significant effect on the ZIF-90 crystal structure and further proved that the synthesis of both ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 is successful [47]. Meanwhile, effect of the addition amounts of CDs (0, 5, 10, 15, 20 mg) on the crystal structure of resulting CDs@ZIF-90 composites was also studied by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2b, the addition of CDs hardly influenced the crystallinity of ZIF-90. The inset illustrated the pictures of the composite CDs@ZIF-90 under natural light after the addition of different amounts of CDs, and it can be noticed that the color of the composite gradually deepens as the amount of CDs increased. Fig. S2 displayed fluorescence emission spectra of ZIF-90 doped different amounts of CDs. It was obviously observed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 was the strongest when CDs was added 10 mg.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 were shown in Fig. 2c. It can be seen that the specific surface area slightly decreased from 557.11 m2/g to 527.91 m2/g and the average pore size decreased from 0.51 nm to 0.42 nm after CDs modification. They further demonstrated the successful introduction of CDs into the channels of ZIF-90. The chemical structures of CDs, ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 were further characterized by using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometry in Fig. 2d. In FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs@ZIF-90, the peak at ∼1256 cm−1 belonged to the C–N stretching vibration, the peaks at ∼1360 and ∼1457 cm−1 were attributed to the scissoring and symmetric bending vibrations of Si–C bond, the band at ∼1653 cm−1 was caused by the CO stretching vibration and the strong band at ∼1100 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of Si-OR or Si=O [46]. Simultaneously, comparing the FT-IR spectra of ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90, it can be found that some characteristic peaks attributed to the aldehyde group in ZIF-90 also appeared in the spectra of the composite, such as ∼1668, ∼1448 and ∼950 cm−1 [48]. Obviously, IR spectra provided further evidence for the successful preparation of ZIF-90, CDs and CDs@ZIF-90. To further determine the surface chemical composition of CDs@ZIF-90, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs, ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 were performed and the results were shown in Fig. 2e. The survey spectrum of CDs@ZIF-90 indicated the presence of C, N, O, Si and Zn elements in the composite material. A characteristic peak of Si 2p is evidently distinguishable in CDs@ZIF-90. Particularly, Fig. 2f, Si 2p high-resolution spectrum, more distinctly proved existence of Si element in CDs@ZIF-90, which further indicated that CDs has been successfully introduced into ZIF-90. In addition, TGA analysis was carried out on ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 under N2 protection for evaluating their thermal stability. Fig. S3 exhibited that these two materials experienced similar weight-loss process with three weight-loss stages. The initial weight loss before 120 °C can be attributed to the loss of organic solvents adsorbed within the structure. The weight loss in the range of 120 °C–430 °C was ascribed to the decomposition of residual organic ligands. The mass reduction after 430 °C was due to the collapse of CDs@ZIF-90 framework. The similar weight loss trend of the CDs@ZIF-90 and ZIF-90 implied that the introduction of the right amount of CDs didn't lower significantly thermal stability of the MOF. Such a high thermal decomposition temperature (430 °C) indicates that these two materials have excellent thermal stability.

The CDs@ZIF-90 composite was prepared by encapsulating CDs into ZIF-90 with a simple in-situ growth method. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, under an excitation wavelength of 370 nm with a slit of 5 nm, CDs@ZIF-90 produced the emission at 453 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, ZIF-90 and CDs@ZIF-90 suspensions were shown in Fig. 3b. It was observed that pure ZIF-90 had a weak and wide emission peak in the range 400–550 nm, while CDs displayed a strong fluorescence emission peak at 453 nm. Particularly, when CDs were introduced into ZIF-90, the fluorescence intensity of the resulting composite increased 57.6 % compared with primitive CDs but their emission peak positions were identical to each other. Evidently this blue fluorescence was attributed to the characteristic emission of CDs centers. The composite emitted stronger fluorescence possibly because the introduction of CDs into ZIF-90 reduced the agglomeration effect of CDs.

In order to study the optical stability of CDs@ZIF-90, its fluorescence emission spectra under various extreme conditions were investigated. Firstly, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-90 at different pH values were recorded in Fig. S4a. It can be observed that the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 basically remained unchanged with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.1 % in the pH range from 4 to 11 in Fig. S4b, indicating that the material was suitable to being used in both acidic and alkaline environments with an excellent optical stability. In addition, when it was stored for different days it was found that its fluorescence intensity daily almost kept constant (Fig. S5), indicating excellent storage stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Al3+ and Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility of the CDs@ZIF-90 system as a fluorescence sensor for Al3+ and Hg2+ detection, 0.5 mg of CDs@ZIF-90 was respectively dispersed in a series of 5 mL of aqueous solutions containing different metal ions (Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Na+ and K+, 1 × 10−2 M) by 5 min sonication and then the emission spectra of the resulting mixtures were recorded at 453 nm. It was observed that the fluorescence intensities of different metal ion solutions were significantly different in Fig. 4 a. Hg2+ almost completely quenched fluorescence emission of the system whereas Al3+ significantly enhanced the fluorescence of the system. Fig. 4b illustrated the histogram of the relative emission intensity I/I0 versus different metal ions, and it can be seen that I/I0 decreased dramatically from the original 1.0 to 0.055 when Hg2+ was present, whereas the intensity increased significantly from the original 1.0 to 6.77 after the addition of Al3+. Simultaneously, the introduction of Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Na+, Ag+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Hg2+ and Co2+ resulted in negligible changes to the fluorescence intensity of the sensor. To enhance the clarity of results, a comparison was made between the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-90 suspensions after addition of Al3+ and Hg2+, respectively (Fig. 4c). As seen from the inset, the addition of Al3+ significantly amplified the blue fluorescence of the solution under UV-lamp, whereas Hg2+ drastically attenuated the fluorescence of the CDs@ZIF-90 suspension. Therefore, the naked eye can clearly discern the change in fluorescence color under UV-lamp upon introduction of the target ions. In addition, it can be still observed that the addition of Hg2+ caused the specific coordinates in the CIE chromaticity diagram to move from the blue region (0.1499, 0.1322) to the light blue region (0.1786 0.2761). The presence of Al3+ caused the coordinates to move from the blue region (0.1499, 0.1322) to the dark blue region (0.1432, 0.1012) in Fig. 4d. The above results demonstrated that the CDs@ZIF-90 dual-function sensor had a high specificity for both Al3+ and Hg2+ detection. Fig. S6 exhibited that the relative fluorescence strengths of CDs@ZIF-90 in Hg2+-Mn+ and Al3+-Mn + mixed metal ion systems were nearly equivalent to those in pure Hg2+ and Al3+ solutions, respectively, and the possible interference substances did not affect the selective responses of CDs@ZIF-90 to Hg2+ and Al3+ at 453 nm, indicating that the recognition of Al3+ and Hg2+ in aqueous solution by CDs@ZIF-90 was not interfered by other possible metal ions.

To investigate the sensitivity of CDs@ZIF-90 to the detection of the target analytes, the following experiments were carried out. Firstly, the impact of different concentrations of Al3+ on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 at 453 nm gradually increased with the increase of Al3+ concentration. Particularly, the fluorescence intensity ratio (I/I0) exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of Al3+ in the range of 1–200 μM (Fig. 5b) with a linear regression equation I/I0 = 2.07 × 10−2CAl+1.00 (R2 = 0.994). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.81 μM based on the equation LOD = 3S/K (where S was the blank samples' standard deviation, and K represented the calibration curve's slope). Fig. 5e also showed that with increasing concentration, the CIE coordinate moved uniformly to the dark blue region. The performance of this sensor to detect Al3+ ions was compared with previously reported MOF-based fluorescent sensors in Table S1, and the results showed that it had a lower detection limit and a wider linear range. The finding demonstrated that CDs@ZIF-90 can not only qualitatively recognize Al3+, but also quantitatively detect Al3+ in aqueous solutions.

The developed fluorescent probe can also be used to achieve multiplex detection of Hg2+ ions. As depicted in Fig. 5c and d, the fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing Hg2+ ion concentration and its relative intensity also showed a good linear relationship with the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.05–240 μM. The linear regression equation could be expressed as I0/I = 1.69 × 10−2CHg + 1.00 and the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.992. LOD was calculated to be 19.6 nM. In addition, with Hg2+ concentration increasing, the CIE coordinate exhibited a uniform shift towards the light blue region, as illustrated in Fig. 5f. The LOD of the sensor CDs@ZIF-90 is significantly lower than the World Health Organization (WHO) allowable level (30 nM) [49], so the sensor can be employed for the detection of Hg2+ in drinking water samples. At the same time, by comparison it was known tht the sensor also had a comaprable or even lower LOD than most reported similar sensors (Table S2).

In addition, the effect of the simultaneous presence of Hg2+ and Al3+ on the mutual detection of each other was explored. The fluorescence responses of several available Hg2+ and Al3+ masking agents including I−, cysteine (Cys); citric acid (CA), tartaric acid (TA), F−, EDTA, SO3 2− to CDs@ZIF-90 were explored in Fig. 6 a. It can be found that I−, F− and SO3 2− nearly did not influence the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90, whereas other selected masking agents significantly enhanced its fluorescence intensity. Therefore, it was acceptable to choose F−, I−, and SO3 2− as candidate masking agents for Hg2+ and Al3+ ions. Next, the amount of masking agent was explored and optimized in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8. For the determination of Al3+, Hg2+ was complexed with I− to mask the interference. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture of Al3+ and Hg2+ was lower than that of the pure Al3+ solution but the fluorescence intensity increased upon addition of I− up to 200 μM then decreased at higher concentrations (Figs.S7a and S7b), so the optimal concentration of masking agent I− toward Hg2+ was 200 μM. At the same time, for the determination of Hg2+, Al3+ was complexed with F− to mask the interference. The addition of the masking agent F− weakened the fluorescence intensity of the system in Fig. S8a. However, the intensity did not change longer when the concentration exceeded 500 μM in Fig. S8b. Therefore, the optimal concentration should be 500 μM when choosing the masking agent F− for Al3+. Fig. 6b exhibited the fluorescent intensity change of the CDs@ZIF-90 suspension in the presence of different masking agents when equal amounts of Hg2+ and Al3+ were added. It was observed that the addition of I−, a masking agent for Hg2+, resulted in an observable enhancement in fluorescence intensity, indicating that the combining I− with Hg2+ further mitigated the quenching effect on the system and I− was able to effectively mask the interference of Hg2+ to Al3+ detection. At this time, Hg2+ was coordinated by I− to form HgI2, so that the fluorescence of the system was mainly dominated by Al3+. On the other hand, when F− was added it could coordinate with Al3+ to form stable AlFx 3−x species so that interference of Al3+ to Hg2+ detection was diminished. From the above results, it can be known that when Hg2+ and Al3+ coexisted, they can be individually detected by adding related masking agent, which also further proved that the sensor had excellent practical application value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent test strips for detection of Hg2+ and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared test strips were used for the visual detection of different concentrations of Al3+ and Hg2+ as shown in Fig. 7 . It was evident that the brightness of the blue fluorescence test strip gradually increased with increasing concentration of Al3+ ions, but in Hg2+ solution the result was just opposite. These results were consistent with those in solution media, indicating that the test strip can be exploited to detect effectively both Al3+ and Hg2+, which provided a valuable assistance for on-site detection of corresponding species in environmental water samples. However, the detection limits (10 μM) of the test strip method were obviously higher than those of corresponding liquid phase approach, indicating that the proposed test strip method was only suitable for high mercury or high aluminum concentration of water sample.

The constructed fluorescent probe was successfully applied in the determination of Al3+ and Hg2+ in the actual samples. As shown in Table S3, the recoveries of Al3+ and Hg2+ at different spiked concentrations (10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM) ranged from 98.14 %–103.3 % and 98.77%–101.3 % for the Yellow River water samples and 100.5 %–102.1 % and 99.26%–101.7 % for the tap water samples, respectively (The recovery rate was calculated by equation R% = Cd/Cx × 100 %, where Cd (μM) and Cs (μM) are the detected concentration and spiked concentration, respectively [50]). These results demonstrated that the analytical results were accurate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the fluorescence response
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the sensing mechanism of CDs@ZIF-90 probe towards Al3+ and Hg2+, we compared the XRD spectra of the sample before and after treatment with Al3+ and Hg2+, and the results were illustrated in Fig. S9. Meanwhile, the morphological changes of the samples before and after the treatment of Al3+ and Hg2+ respectively were compared by SEM in Fig. S10. It was found that addition of Al3+ and Hg2+ did not alter the structure and morphological of CDs@ZIF-90. Therefore, the possibility the collapse of the ZIF-90 structure causes fluorescence decrease or enhancement can be ruled out.

To investigate the interactions between these two metal ions and CDs@ZIF-90, the Mn + -CDs@ZIF-90 fluorescence composite was further studied by acid-base titration experiments. When different concentrations of HCl solution were added to 10 mL of the suspension of CDs@ZIF-90 containing Al3+, a decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing HCl concentration was observed in Fig. S11a. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased again with the addition of different concentrations of NaOH solution (Fig. S11b). The converse phenomenon was observed upon addition of HCl and NaOH to the suspension of CDs@ZIF-90 containing Al3+ ions. As shown in Fig. 8 a, it was clearly observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase of HCl concentration while the subsequent addition of NaOH enhanced the fluorescence of the system again. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the addition of NaOH solution in Hg2+-CDs@ZIF-90 (Fig. S11c), and then gradually increased with subsequent addition of HCl solution (Fig. S11d). The addition of HCl and NaOH caused an opposite trend in the fluorescence intensity of system Hg2+-CDs@ZIF-90 as seen in the scatter plot Fig. 8b. These results strongly verified that the CDs@ZIF-90 combined with Al3+ or Hg2+ through a reversible Lewis acid-base association between free N atoms in CDs@ZIF-90 and the metal ions [51].

Afterwards, the UV absorption spectrum of the Hg2+ solution and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CD@ZIF-90 were also recorded. A clear overlap between these two spectra was observed in Fig. S12. These results suggest that the fluorescence quenching of CD@ZIF-90 caused by Hg2+ is due to the internal filtration effect (IFE) [52]. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 was significantly reduced by the addition of Hg2+ ions. It was speculated that it might be caused by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [53]. The CDs@ZIF-90 contains not only N from CDs, but also N from imidazole in the ligand. Therefore, the lone pair electrons of N atom in CDs@ZIF-90 cause a strong coordination with Hg2+. The electron transfers to the vacant orbital of Hg2+ to form a metal complex under excitation state. In addition, the zeta potentials of CD@ZIF-90 were compared before and after treatment with Hg2+ in Fig. S13. After adding Hg2+ to CDs@ZIF-90, the zeta potential changed from +8.6 mV to +21.1 mV, suggesting that electron transfer occurred between Hg2+ and the functional groups on the surface of CDs@ZIF-90 and electrons transferred from the CDs@ZIF-90 to the electron-deficient Hg2+, which further indicated that PET between Hg and CDs@ZIF-90 also caused the fluorescence quenching [54]. Consequently, the presence of Hg2+ leads to fluorescence quenching a by a combination of IFE and PET mechanisms.

Obviously, the imidazole N atom in the ligand has the potential to interact with the metal ions. To further explore the possible mechanism underlying the turn-on effect, UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@ZIF-90, CDs@ZIF-90 + different concentrations of Al3+ and Al3+ aqueous solutions were measured and depicted in Fig. 9 . Notably, CDs@ZIF-90 exhibits a strong absorption band within the range of 250–325 nm and a weak absorption band in 325–400 nm. When low concentration of Al3+ (5.0 × 10−5 mol/L) was added the intensity of the former band obviously increased whereas the latter slightly decreased. But when high concentration of Al3+ existed, the former slightly decreased whereas the latter significantly increased. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the interaction between the N atoms in imidazole and Al3+. Noticably, Al3+ solution did not show any perceptible absorption in the observed wavelength range, which confirmed that the amplified absorbance of CDs@ZIF-90 after Al3+ addition did not originate from the metal ion itself. The interaction between CDs@ZIF-90 and Al3+ results in a significant enhancement of absorbance, further increasing luminescence intensity. As a result, the fluorescence “turn-on” mechanism of CDs@ZIF-90 for Al3+ can be attributed to the absorbance caused enhancement (ACE) [55,56]. To affirm above conclusion, we studied effect of Al3+ concentration on UV–Vis absorption intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 was investigated. As shown in Fig. S14 with increasing Al3+ concentration up to 3.0 × 10−4 mol/L the absorbance peak intensity of CDs@ZIF-90 at 287 nm gradually increased whereas the intesity at 370 nm correspondingly reduced. When Al3+ concentration continued to increase from 4.0 × 10−4 mol/L the absorption peak intensity at 370 nm started to increase with increasing Al3+ cocentration. This phenomenon probaly implied that Al3+ formed different ratios of species with CDs@ZIF-90 as Al3+ concentration increased. Considering the fact that both 287 nm and 370 nm can excite fluorescence emission of the probe material, the above ACE mechanism is rationable.

In summary, a dual-functional fluorescent probe was constructed for the specific detection of Al3+ and Hg2+ ions in water samples. Thereinto, the presence of Al3+ ions made the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-90 enhance due to ACE, while the presence of Hg2+ caused its fluorescence to decrease by the IFE and PET process between Hg2+ and CDs@ZIF-90. As a result, the developed probe realized turn-on mode quantification of Al3+ in the range of 1–200 μM with an LOD of 0.81 μM and turn-off mode detection of Hg2+ in the range of 0.05–240 μM with an LOD of 19.6 nM. At the same time, portable test strips were also successfully prepared for the qualitative and visual detection of the targeted ions in environmental water samples. Considering high sensitivity and excellent selectivity, the sensor has a great potential in environmental water quality monitoring for protection of human health.
